ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Malpua exists; closest match is 'pancake food product' (FOODON_03301710). Malpua is a deep-fried, syrup-soaked Indian sweet pancake distinct from Western pancakes.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Sweet pastries and cakes) is the closest defensible category for Malpua. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for this traditional Indian sweet pancake.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Malpua or equivalent traditional Indian sweet pancake.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Malpua is one piece weighing approximately 120 grams, providing around 462 kcal, and is commonly served warm with Rabri (thickened sweetened milk) or on its own as a festive sweet.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Malpua is not listed as a standalone entry in ICMR-NIN tables. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for deep-fried wheat-flour-based Indian sweets (e.g., gulab jamun, jalebi) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for similar fried dough products soaked in sugar syrup. Saturated fat estimated from ghee content (~40% of total fat). Cholesterol estimated from ghee and khoya contribution. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown is not available in approved sources for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on refined wheat flour fried dough products soaked in sugar syrup
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Malpua exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is estimated by analogy with refined wheat flour deep-fried dough products soaked in concentrated sugar syrup (e.g., jalebi GI ~65–80, gulab jamun GI ~70–75 range) as reported in peer-reviewed literature and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × (carbohydrate_g - dietary_fibre_g) per serving) / 100 = (72 × (52.0 - 1.2) × 1.2) / 100 = 43.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High caloric density and saturated fat from ghee frying; moderate sodium content
hypertension.guidance: Malpua is calorie-dense (385 kcal/100g) with approximately 10.2g saturated fat per 100g from ghee; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to occasional small servings and monitor overall saturated fat and caloric intake to avoid weight gain, which is a primary driver of elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI ~72), very high sugar content (35g/100g), and refined carbohydrate load from maida and sugar syrup
type2_diabetes.guidance: Malpua has a high glycemic index (~72) and delivers approximately 35g of sugar per 100g, causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit consumption, and if consumed, pair with a protein or fat source to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from ghee (~10.2g/100g), high sugar content, and caloric density contributing to dyslipidaemia and weight gain
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high saturated fat from ghee frying (~10.2g/100g) and high added sugar (35g/100g) makes Malpua a high cardiovascular risk food; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional indulgence only, limiting intake to small portions infrequently and prioritising heart-healthy dietary patterns otherwise.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory ingredients: refined flour, concentrated sugar, and deep-fried preparation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Refined wheat flour, high sugar, and deep-frying in ghee are associated with pro-inflammatory dietary patterns; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimize Malpua intake to avoid potential exacerbation of inflammatory symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Malpua is a traditional Indian sweet pancake with centuries of culinary history, prominently featured during festivals such as Holi, Eid, and Diwali, and served as a sacred offering at the Jagannath Temple in Odisha. The batter is prepared from refined wheat flour (maida) and semolina (suji), enriched with milk solids (khoya) or mashed banana for richness and a soft, fluffy interior texture. Aromatic spices including fennel seeds and green cardamom are incorporated into the batter. Portions of batter are deep-fried in ghee until the edges turn golden and crisp while the centre remains tender and spongy. The freshly fried pancakes are immediately immersed in a warm, fragrant sugar syrup (chashni) infused with cardamom and saffron, which saturates the pancake and gives it its characteristic sweetness and glossy appearance. In North India, Malpua is classically served topped with chilled Rabri — a slow-cooked thickened sweetened milk — creating a contrast of warm, crisp pancake against cold, creamy topping. Nutritionally, Malpua is energy-dense at approximately 385 kcal per 100g, with 18.5g fat (predominantly saturated from ghee), 52g carbohydrates, and 35g sugar. Its high glycemic index (~72) and very high sugar content make it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease as a regular food. It is best enjoyed as an occasional festive treat in small portions.

What is the difference between Malpua and jalebi?

meal_context: festive dessert during Holi, Eid, and Diwali
served with Rabri as a North Indian dessert

confidence_reason: Malpua is not listed as a standalone entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for comparable deep-fried Indian wheat-flour sweets and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy fried dough products. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on similar refined-flour fried-dough products soaked in sugar syrup. Saturated fat and cholesterol are estimated from ghee and khoya contribution. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture data for Malpua are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024 for this specific traditional preparation. 2. Saturated fat (10.2g/100g) is estimated based on ghee contributing approximately 55% of total fat and khoya contributing the remainder, using ICMR-NIN fatty acid profiles for ghee. 3. Cholesterol (38mg/100g) is estimated from ghee and khoya content using USDA SR Legacy values for clarified butter and milk solids. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Malpua exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Glycemic load per 120g serving = 43.9. 6. The portions array in the input contained four identical duplicate entries; deduplicated to a single 120g serving. 7. Bengali alias মালপোয়া and Odia alias ମାଲପୁଆ are preserved from input. 8. This is an Indian dish; Singaporean multilingual requirements (Chinese, Malay) do not apply. Hindi and Tamil aliases are included as Malpua is consumed across Hindi-speaking and Tamil-speaking regions of India.
